topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The acceptance letter felt like a physical weight pressing down on Leo’s chest. He stared at the glossy paper, the congratulatory words blurring into meaningless ink. He’d poured every ounce of his energy, every grueling early morning practice, into this moment, into the belief that this was his culmination. Now, the silence of his small apartment was deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming in his ears. He picked up his phone, intending to call his coach, but his thumb hovered uselessly above the dial pad. Every muscle in his neck felt tight, a knot refusing to loosen. He paced the worn floorboards, the rhythm of his steps uneven, a restless energy buzzing beneath his skin. He kept running through the tape in his mind—the missed catches, the shaky free throws—each failure echoing louder than the cheers he’d imagined. When he finally collapsed onto his bed, the sheer volume of expectation, the sudden absence of it, made his breath catch, shallow and ragged.

[story 2]

"No." The word tasted like ash in Maya's mouth as she reread the email for the tenth time. Her hands shook so violently she had to grip the edge of the desk to steady them. The fluorescent lights of the university office seemed too bright, exposing every flicker of doubt in her eyes. She felt a strange detachment, as if she were watching the whole situation unfold from a great distance, unable to truly inhabit the disappointment. She stood up abruptly, knocking her chair slightly askew. The noise seemed to amplify the internal chaos churning within her. She walked to the window, watching the busy street below, the motion of the cars a dizzying blur. A hot flush crept up her neck, and she squeezed her eyes shut, trying to force the churning sensation inside her to subside, but it only intensified, a relentless pressure building behind her sternum.

[story 3]

The team meeting was a study in muted tones. Everyone else seemed composed, their nods and focused expressions creating a wall of calm around the selection process. But for Alex, the air felt thick, suffocating. He kept fiddling with the drawstring of his sweatpants, the rough fabric abrasive against his skin. When the coach finally called his name, the sound didn't register as praise; it registered as a sharp, cold blow. Alex managed a weak smile, a performance that felt brittle and entirely fake. As he walked out of the room, the hallway seemed miles long. His vision tunneled slightly, and he leaned against the cool cinderblock wall, his shoulders slumping forward. He didn't want to speak; the effort required to articulate the churning sense of failure felt monumental. He just wanted to disappear into the anonymity of the corridor, the sheer magnitude of the letdown making his limbs feel heavy, unresponsive.
